steps.title: The Drift Is Silent
steps.body:
Couples don't wake up one day and decide to drift apart. It happens in inches.

A conversation that didn't happen. A bid for connection that got ignored. A resentment that went unspoken. Over months and years, these inches become miles.

John Gottman, who studied thousands of couples over four decades, found something remarkable: he could predict divorce with 94% accuracy by watching just 15 minutes of a couple talking.

The signs are visible. You just have to know what to look for.

The distance between you didn't appear overnight.

It's the sum of a thousand tiny moments where you turned away instead of toward each other.

The good news? You can turn back toward each other starting right now.

steps.title: The Four Horsemen
steps.body:
Gottman identified four communication patterns that predict relationship failure. He called them the Four Horsemen:

1. Criticism — Attacking your partner's character, not their behavior
2. Contempt — The single biggest predictor of divorce. Mockery, eye-rolling, sneering.
3. Defensiveness — Playing the victim, making excuses, counter-attacking
4. Stonewalling — Shutting down, withdrawing, refusing to engage

Every couple does these sometimes. The danger isn't doing them once — it's when they become the default.

steps.question: Which of the Four Horsemen is the single biggest predictor of divorce?
feedback: Correct. Contempt — mockery, eye-rolling, sneering — is the most destructive pattern. It communicates disgust and superiority, making repair nearly impossible.
Not this one. While all four are dangerous, contempt is the most corrosive. It's the one that makes the other three harder to recover from.

steps.title: Recognize Any Horsemen?
steps.prompt:
Which of the Four Horsemen — criticism, contempt, defensiveness, or stonewalling — shows up most often in your relationship?

Don't judge yourself or your partner. Just notice. Awareness is the first step to change.

steps.title: Bids for Connection
steps.body:
A 'bid' is any attempt to connect — a question, a touch, a look, a comment about the weather. They're tiny, easy to miss, and incredibly important.

Gottman found that couples who stay together respond positively to each other's bids 86% of the time.

Couples who divorce? Only 33%.

The difference between thriving and failing isn't the absence of problems. It's the presence of response.

steps.title: The Missed Bid
steps.body:
Your partner says, "Look at that sunset."

You have three choices:

1. Turn toward — look up, engage. "Wow, the colors are amazing tonight."
2. Turn away — ignore it, keep scrolling. Maybe grunt in acknowledgment.
3. Turn against — dismiss it. "I'm busy, can't you see?"

The sunset isn't about the sunset. It's a bid. They're saying: "I want to share a moment with you."

How you respond shapes the next decade of your marriage.

steps.question: Most couples who divorce have more conflicts than couples who stay together.
feedback: Correct. Research shows that happy couples and unhappy couples have about the same number of conflicts. The difference is how they repair afterward. It's not about avoiding fights — it's about recovering from them.
Actually, research shows similar conflict levels. The difference is in how couples respond to bids for connection and how they repair after disagreements.

steps.title: Your Bid Response Rate
steps.prompt:
Think about yesterday. How many times did your partner make a bid for connection — a question, a look, a touch? And how did you respond?

What's one small way you could turn toward a bid today that you might have missed yesterday?

steps.title: Tomorrow: The Foundation Audit
steps.body:
Today you learned why couples drift — the Horsemen that predict failure and the bids that predict success.

Tomorrow, we go deeper. You'll audit your Foundation — the trust, respect, and shared values that everything else rests on.

Until then, here's your mission: notice one bid for connection today and turn toward it. Just one.
